front.title: Deployment Experience and Considerations for Unicast VRRP

abstract.t: This document surveys deployed approaches to operating the Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) over unicast transport in environments where the multicast assumptions of RFC 9568 are unavailable, undesirable, or operationally constrained. The document summarizes implementation patterns seen in public documentation, highlights common configuration and operational themes, and identifies interoperability gaps between deployed approaches.

This document does not define a new interoperable unicast VRRP protocol. Instead, it records deployment experience and outlines the areas that would need to be addressed by any future standards-track extension to VRRP.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: defines version 3 of the Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) for IPv4 and IPv6. VRRP provides a first-hop redundancy mechanism in which multiple routers cooperate so that one router, the Active Router, forwards traffic for a protected virtual router while one or more Backup Routers stand by to take over if needed.

The deployed environment for first-hop redundancy has evolved. Many service-provider, cloud, overlay, and virtualized environments either do not support the multicast delivery model assumed by or do not expose it in a form that is operationally equivalent to a classic shared LAN. As a result, several implementations now support some form of "unicast VRRP".

Public documentation shows that these implementations do not all make the same design choices. Some preserve the traditional VRRP gateway protection model and primarily replace multicast advertisement delivery with configured unicast peers. Others use VRRP-derived state exchange to solve a broader routed active/backup role-selection problem.

The purpose of this document is to capture that deployment experience and describe where the common ground ends. This provides a basis for discussing whether future standardization should focus on a minimal unicast transport extension for VRRP, a richer multi-peer unicast profile, or a separate active/standby election mechanism for routed environments.

section.name: Scope and Non-Goals
section.t: This document is informational. It documents publicly visible industry practice and the resulting design space. It is not a protocol specification and it does not update .
This document has the following goals:

ul.li: describe the multicast and LAN assumptions in ,
summarize deployed unicast VRRP implementation models,
highlight operational and interoperability differences, and
identify the sections of that would likely need revision in a future standards-track effort.

section.t: This document intentionally does not:

ul.li: claim interoperability among deployed unicast implementations,
specify a new wire protocol, or
declare one vendor-specific implementation model to be the standard.

section.name: Standards Baseline
section.t: The starting point for any discussion of unicast VRRP is the protocol model in .

section.name: Assumptions in RFC 9568
section.t: specifies VRRP operation on a shared LAN. VRRP advertisements are sent to the well-known VRRP multicast destination addresses for IPv4 and IPv6, and receive-side processing checks the IPv4 TTL or IPv6 Hop Limit to ensure that the packet has not traversed a remote network.
The protocol is also tightly coupled to the use of a Virtual Router MAC address and to host-facing ARP or Neighbor Discovery behavior associated with first-hop gateway protection on a shared segment.

section.name: Deployment Drivers for Unicast Operation
section.t: Operators increasingly deploy redundant routers in routed, overlay, and virtualized environments where native LAN multicast is not available or is operationally inconvenient. Public cloud is one example, but the details matter. Azure Virtual Network explicitly supports only unicast traffic within the virtual network , while AWS supports multicast through Transit Gateway multicast rather than through the simple on-link model assumed by classic VRRP .

In these environments, operators still want fast failure detection and deterministic active/backup behavior for one or more protected addresses, prefixes, or route-owner roles. This operational demand is the primary reason unicast VRRP variants have emerged.

section.name: Survey of Deployed Unicast VRRP Approaches
section.t: This section summarizes implementation behavior described in publicly available documentation. The absence of a feature in this survey only means that it was not found in the referenced public documentation.

section.name: Common Design Families
section.t: The published implementations reviewed by the authors fit roughly into three families:

li.t: A transport-replacement model in which VRRP largely preserves its role as a first-hop redundancy mechanism while advertisement delivery is changed from multicast to configured unicast peers.
A multi-peer unicast model in which multiple peers can be configured explicitly and additional source or TTL validation controls are provided.
A routed active/backup election model in which VRRP-derived protocol elements are used for role selection across a Layer 3 network and not always for classic host-facing gateway protection.

table.name: Examples of deployed unicast VRRP behavior

tr.td: Preserves VIP-centric first-hop redundancy behavior and disables multicast VRRP when unicast is configured
Documents a proprietary VRRPv2-based unicast mode, configurable UDP port usage, optional authentication, and semantics that differ from common VRRP gateway protection
Supports peer lists, source validation, and TTL controls; also notes that unicast peers are outside strict RFC behavior
Supports explicit peer and source configuration and an optional compatibility mode closer to classic VRRP semantics
Documents unicast VRRP in cloud workflows and uses VRRP state to influence route-table ownership in AWS-oriented deployments
Public documentation reviewed by the authors remains aligned with multicast VRRP behavior
Public documentation reviewed by the authors describes VRRPv2 and VRRPv3 behavior on Linux without documenting a unicast mode

section.name: Configured Peers
section.t: Every deployed unicast approach reviewed by the authors replaces passive discovery through multicast reception with some form of explicit peer configuration. The most basic form is a single configured peer address for a two-node deployment. Other implementations support a list of peers and optional source address configuration.

section.name: Packet Transport
section.t: The reviewed implementations do not all converge on one transport model. Some are described as unicast delivery of VRRP advertisements, while Huawei documents a VRRPv2-based proprietary unicast mode with a configurable UDP port. This is a significant interoperability fault line because not all implementations appear to preserve the exact packet and transport assumptions of .

section.name: TTL and Hop-Limit Validation
section.t: The receive-side IPv4 TTL or IPv6 Hop Limit check in is a core part of VRRP's LAN-scoped security model. Deployed unicast implementations preserve this logic to varying degrees. Some preserve the value 255 check, some expose minimum and maximum TTL controls, and some rely more heavily on source address validation because the protected peers are no longer confined to one simple shared segment.

section.name: Virtual MAC, ARP, and Neighbor Discovery Behavior
section.t: Deployed implementations differ on whether unicast VRRP is still fundamentally a host-facing first-hop redundancy mechanism. In the conservative transport-replacement model, the Virtual Router MAC address, ARP behavior, gratuitous ARP, and IPv6 Neighbor Discovery logic remain important. In more routed active/backup models, the role of a Virtual Router MAC address may be reduced or the first-hop gateway semantics may no longer be the primary objective.

section.name: VIP Protection versus Role Selection
section.t: The most important semantic split in the deployment space is whether "unicast VRRP" is being used to protect a virtual first-hop address or to perform a broader active/backup role election across a Layer 3 topology. The former use case is much closer to the original VRRP model. The latter use case may still reuse VRRP concepts and state, but it stretches beyond the assumptions of .

section.name: Management Considerations
section.t: Publicly documented unicast implementations expose additional knobs beyond those needed for multicast VRRP. Examples include peer lists, source-address pinning, TTL or hop-limit validation policy, transport selection, and unicast-specific authentication. These are not modeled by the current base VRRP specification and would need explicit treatment in any future management model update.

section.name: Interoperability Considerations
section.t: This document does not assume that the deployed implementations summarized above interoperate on the wire. In fact, the reviewed public documentation strongly suggests that interoperability should not be assumed.
The main interoperability gaps are:

ul.li: different receive-side source and TTL validation models,
different expectations around the Virtual Router MAC address, ARP, and Neighbor Discovery, and
different problem statements, namely first-hop gateway protection versus routed active/backup role selection.

section.t: For that reason, the most accurate near-term framing for unicast VRRP is a family of deployment techniques rather than a single interoperable extension of .

section.name: Considerations for Future Standardization
section.t: The deployment experience summarized in this document suggests three possible directions for future work:

li.t: A minimal extension that preserves VRRP packet semantics and first-hop redundancy behavior while replacing multicast delivery with configured unicast peers.
A richer unicast VRRP profile that standardizes peer lists, source validation, and multi-peer behavior.
A separate active/backup election mechanism for routed environments, if the working group concludes that the routed role selection use case should not be forced into the classic VRRP model.

section.t: Whichever direction is chosen, it would need to make an explicit decision about scope. A future document should not try to standardize all observed deployment styles at once unless it is prepared to define multiple profiles with clear interoperability expectations.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: relies in part on the IPv4 TTL or IPv6 Hop Limit value of 255 to keep attacks scoped to the local network . Moving VRRP-like behavior to unicast environments can change that threat model, especially when peers are separated by one or more routed hops or when the protected role is no longer limited to host-facing gateway service on a LAN.

Publicly documented implementations respond to this in different ways, including explicit peer allow-lists, configured source addresses, TTL or hop-limit range checks, and vendor-specific authentication. These mitigations improve robustness, but they also show that deployed unicast approaches do not currently share a single security model.

Any future standards-track unicast VRRP specification would need to define a precise peer trust model, specify how receive-side validation is performed, and describe what protections are expected when the protocol operates beyond a single shared LAN.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

section.name: Appendix A. Candidate RFC 9568 Touchpoints for a Future Protocol Specification
section.t: The brainstorming for this work identified several parts of that would likely need review if the community later decides to standardize a unicast extension rather than only document deployment experience.

section.name: Definitions
section.t: defines the Virtual Router MAC address in terms that are tightly linked to VRRP advertisement delivery. A future unicast protocol specification would likely need to clarify whether the Virtual Router MAC address remains mandatory, optional, or orthogonal to advertisement delivery.

section.name: Overview and Protocol Description
section.t: and explicitly describe VRRP in multicast terms. A future standards-track document would need to state whether unicast operation is a separate mode, whether it changes the topology assumptions, and what remains common across multicast and unicast operation.

section.name: IPv4 and IPv6 Field Descriptions
section.t: The brainstormed protocol impact points align with and . A future protocol specification would need to revisit destination addressing, source-address selection, and how the TTL or Hop Limit checks apply in routed or peer-configured deployments.

section.name: Transmit Rules
section.t: instructs a sender to transmit to the VRRP IPv4 or IPv6 multicast group. Any interoperable unicast protocol specification would need to replace this with clear peer-selection and destination-addressing rules.

front.title: Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol (VRRP) Version 3 for IPv4 and IPv6
Implementing VRRP on Cisco IOS XR

reference.refcontent: Includes a section on Unicast VRRP

front.title: Cloud-Native Router Deployment on Amazon EKS
